policy_statement: School infrastructure: install water fountains in all school buildings and install rainwater recovery systems in schools with vegetable gardens; develop sustainable water management initiatives in schools.
mechanism_description: The City Council is committed to equipping school buildings with drinking fountains and adding rainwater recovery and infiltration measures so schools can rely less on potable water and better manage stormwater and flooding.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavier rainfall) → stormwater retained/infiltrated/reused → ↓ runoff peak flows & sewer overload → ↓ flood exposure and impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system/activity: Schools are retrofitted with rainwater recovery (cisterns/barrels) and infiltration/green stormwater measures (e.g., soakaways, permeable areas, infiltration trenches), alongside drinking fountains to reduce reliance on bottled/potable supply for some uses.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Capturing rainfall and increasing infiltration/temporary storage reduces the volume and rate of stormwater entering drains and waterways, lowering peak discharge and combined sewer overflow risk; reuse also substitutes some potable demand.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Improved resilience to intensified precipitation events (less localized flooding and overflow), with a secondary co-benefit of reduced energy use and emissions from potable water treatment/pumping where applicable.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood and stormwater damage by lowering runoff volumes/peak flows and increasing on-site retention/infiltration during extreme precipitation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Managing stormwater through capture/infiltration primarily reduces exposure to flooding from heavier rainfall—an adaptation outcome.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While potable water demand reduction can save energy/embedded emissions (resource efficiency/mitigation co-benefit), those savings are typically smaller and highly dependent on local water system energy intensity; flood-risk reduction is the more direct and generally larger climate-relevant effect for stormwater management measures.  
3) Edge cases applied: In arid regions or very energy-intensive desalination systems, potable demand reduction could dominate; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly includes “stormwater management … flood mitigation,” which anchors the dominant effect in resilience to rainfall extremes.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism includes stormwater capture/infiltration for flood mitigation, which consistently maps to adaptation via reduced runoff and flood exposure, even if potable-demand impacts vary.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (reduced treatment/pumping energy, reduced bottled water) should be assessed at the individual policy level; only the primary category is assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implemented solely for water-supply substitution (with negligible flood benefit) or in contexts where potable water is extremely energy/carbon intensive (e.g., desalination-dominant supply), the dominant effect could shift toward Resource Efficiency/Mitigation; otherwise, flood-risk reduction remains primary.
key_indicators: "installing rainwater recovery systems"; "equipping all its school buildings with water fountains"; "rainwater tanks"; "infiltration and flooding zones in courtyards"; "Develop sustainable water management initiatives in schools"
co_benefits: Reduced potable water demand; reduced bottled water use/waste; potential energy/GHG reductions from lower water treatment and pumping; educational/behavioral benefits for students
instance_edge_case_notes: Drinking fountains alone are not necessarily climate-specific, but in combination with rainwater recovery/infiltration measures and the stated “sustainable water management initiatives,” the overall package is best classified as direct adaptation-focused infrastructure investment.
